text: This video is a demonstration of a laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy of a persistent simple cyst. The patient is a thirty-two year old female with a three year history of primary infertility. The four centimeter left ovarian simple cyst has been unchanged for at least two years. The patient has also had progressive dysmenorrhea suggestive of endometriosis and a laparoscopy was planned to assess and treat the endometriosis and the decision was made to remove the cyst at the same time.
The laparoscopic view reveals the left ovary containing a four-centimeter simple cyst. Extensive stage two endometriosis is visible throughout the pelvic region.

The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to make an incision at the thinnest area of the ovary's surface, applying a pure cutting current to open the ovary and attempt to reach the plane between the ovarian cyst and the ovary wall.

The surgeon uses right-angled forceps to open the plane between the ovarian cyst and the ovary. The outer ovarian wall is incised until the thicker, wider area of the ovarian cortex is visible, and the cyst is incidentally entered.
The surgeon drains the fluid from the ovarian cyst and performs copious irrigation of the pelvis. The outer ovarian wall is further opened to facilitate the cystectomy.

Maryland dissectors are used to grasp the ovarian cyst wall, while another grasper holds the remainder of the ovary cortex. The surgeon performs blunt dissection to gently and carefully separate the cyst wall from the ovary wall.
The surgeon uses graspers to reposition and provide counter traction near the dissection plane, applying gentle traction to separate the ovarian cyst from the ovary wall.

The surgeon uses pinpoint monopolar cautery to cauterize thicker bands of tissue, while continuing the dissection around the edge of the ovarian cyst wall as it is progressively freed from the surrounding ovarian tissue.

The surgeon continues the dissection around the entire ovarian cyst, holding the cyst wall and carefully dissecting the ovarian cortex away from the cyst. Maintaining the proper dissection plane minimizes blood loss.

The surgeon carefully dissects the ovarian cyst, avoiding excessive pulling to prevent tearing of the ovarian cortex. In cases where the cyst is densely adherent, the cyst wall is opened to facilitate dissection of a single layer away from the ovarian cortex.

The surgeon assesses that further removal of the cyst from the ovarian cortex would cause significant damage. The majority of the cyst has been excised, and the remaining one-centimeter portion of the cyst wall is ablated with electrocautery to avoid harm to the ovarian cortex.

The surgeon inspects and irrigates the ovarian bed, which appears nearly fully hemostatic with minimal use of electrocautery. To reduce adhesions, the outer edges of the ovary are re-approximated using a running forovicral suture.

The surgeon secures the suture and loosely re-approximates the edges of the ovarian bed. The internal surfaces of the bed remain unsutured due to their fully hemostatic condition, and the suture is tied off.
The surgeon wraps the ovary in a sheet of interseed following irrigation and completion of other procedures, aiming to reduce further adhesion formation. The procedure is then concluded.
